tweets.text:
A reminder of what the US waiver revocation on Iranian oil sales means -- and doesn't mean: 

Iran can still sell its oil if it finds a willing buyers (and China has been in the past, and will be again). Yes, Iran will need to discount its oil, but that would be mostly offset by the rise in oil prices triggered by the fresh hostilities. The biggest problem for Iran? It would have to use (again) non-US dollar settlement.

Put it all together, and Tehran would be able to sell the ~60 million barrels it has put on the water so far since the blockade was lifted, at relatively good prices (~$65-70 a barrel), making probably ~$4 billion. 

Only a return to the US Navy blockade would stop Iran from selling its oil. But that means a new war.
tweets.full_text:
A reminder of what the US waiver revocation on Iranian oil sales means -- and doesn't mean: 

Iran can still sell its oil if it finds a willing buyers (and China has been in the past, and will be again). Yes, Iran will need to discount its oil, but that would be mostly offset by the rise in oil prices triggered by the fresh hostilities. The biggest problem for Iran? It would have to use (again) non-US dollar settlement.

Put it all together, and Tehran would be able to sell the ~60 million barrels it has put on the water so far since the blockade was lifted, at relatively good prices (~$65-70 a barrel), making probably ~$4 billion. 

Only a return to the US Navy blockade would stop Iran from selling its oil. But that means a new war.

tweets.text:
Brent crude is currently trading in the mid-$70s, with WTI above $70. (Bloomberg data below).
This comes as traders in Asia react to the latest exchange of fire between the US and Iran.
The dominant view in markets is that this latest flare-up will remain contained. While the situation may reoccur periodically, the prevailing belief is that it will not evolve into a broader conflict.
Should this view be challenged in a significant way, however, oil prices could quickly jump into the $80s or $90s.
#economy #markets #oil #energy

tweets.text:
Bond vol, fed reserves, nfci, usdcnh, cb balance sheets yoy growth + global m2 minus global gdp yoy which is meant to be a proxy for ‘excess’ (aware this latter input is itself a DXY proxy on a daily scale but the model is designed for monthly reads anyway — hence why the ‘live’ daily reading is much more messy and subject to volatility, but still useful for event studies) 

All of this is also rate of change not level
tweets.full_text:
Bond vol, fed reserves, nfci, usdcnh, cb balance sheets yoy growth + global m2 minus global gdp yoy which is meant to be a proxy for ‘excess’ (aware this latter input is itself a DXY proxy on a daily scale but the model is designed for monthly reads anyway — hence why the ‘live’ daily reading is much more messy and subject to volatility, but still useful for event studies) 

All of this is also rate of change not level

tweets.text:
Hesai Technology, a Chinese lidar maker faces US national scrutiny over its expanded partnership with $NVDA and lidar sensors. 

For $OUST, $AEVA, and Western lidar bros, this is generally positive if competitors get regulated out.

Since there were warnings that: 

Sensors could be disabled or exploited remotely, given Hesai firmware update disabled lidar units on February 29 (as evidence). 

By second order effect, this is also bullish for upstream laser suppliers too like $LITE and $SIVE that are used in western lidar players.

For $OUST, $AEVA, and Western lidar bros, this is generally positive if competitors get regulated out.

thread_tweets.text:
Basically:

> major Chinese lidar player might be banned in West. 
> $OUST, $AEVA, and others benefit by filling in the vacuum 
> optical players players that power western lidar players like Aeva also benefit from increased revenue

Not sure if this news really matters given everything is crashing though.

tweets.text:
Just Bloomberg and $META doing damage control after crashing the market with Meta Compute framing:

Spokesperson: "Meta is still hungry for even more computing power. 

It is still moving forward with plans for expensive new data centers and recently inked major computing deals with $CRVW, Google, $ORCL, and others."

Just dropped that in with the Meta Muse announcement, and evenn threw in the "expensive" framing with DCs to signal capex. 

But little late given we're likely seeing a lot of margin liquidation cascades and heavy losses from media framing earlier.

tweets.text:
Ari’s take is that AI development will turn into a Los Alamos style project, which sounds crazy but we’ve done it before. 

We quite literally put nuclear scientists in the desert and controlled their entire existence. 

The main difference now is just that the private sector built the technology first so the public is aware. 

His bet is AI gets the same treatment inside 3 years. Government phones for researchers, travel with permission, etc.

Personally think it’s a bit extreme but worth running the thought experiment.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Nobody is talking about AI nationalization.

AI is not just a technology, it is a military project. According to @AriDavidPaul, we could see a Los Alamos style lockdown of the AI Labs in the distant future.

What trades are downstream from government takeover? https://t.co/W2UkiUPgvH
tweets.full_text:
Ari’s take is that AI development will turn into a Los Alamos style project, which sounds crazy but we’ve done it before. 

We quite literally put nuclear scientists in the desert and controlled their entire existence. 

The main difference now is just that the private sector built the technology first so the public is aware. 

His bet is AI gets the same treatment inside 3 years. Government phones for researchers, travel with permission, etc.

Personally think it’s a bit extreme but worth running the thought experiment.

tweets.text:
How to destroy a city.

Win an election by promising half the tenants frozen rents paid for by increased rents on the rest of the city’s tenants who often live in the same buildings with their subsidized neighbors. Pit neighbor against neighbor. 

Create a city hostile to developers of apartments eliminating the potential for new supply. 

Freeze rents so it is cheaper not to rent the apartment than to spend the capital required to renovate and rent it, shrinking the supply further. 

Place all of the increased burden on the unregulated, market-rate tenants and the owners of buildings with no market rate apartments that can’t raise rents forcing them into foreclosure and the buildings into disrepair. 

That’s all you need to do.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
We're going to be seeing more and more of this -- NYC rent is about to skyrocket for market-rate tenants in  buildings with a mix of stabilized and market-rate units. 

The mayor created a system in which neighbors are forced to subsidize neighbors -- irrespective of income.
tweets.full_text:
How to destroy a city.

Win an election by promising half the tenants frozen rents paid for by increased rents on the rest of the city’s tenants who often live in the same buildings with their subsidized neighbors. Pit neighbor against neighbor. 

Create a city hostile to developers of apartments eliminating the potential for new supply. 

Freeze rents so it is cheaper not to rent the apartment than to spend the capital required to renovate and rent it, shrinking the supply further. 

Place all of the increased burden on the unregulated, market-rate tenants and the owners of buildings with no market rate apartments that can’t raise rents forcing them into foreclosure and the buildings into disrepair. 

That’s all you need to do.

tweets.text:
In addition to absolutely crushing the English in the Viking age, the Norwegians also massively owned the English in polar expeditions.

Here is the story of Amundsen v Scott.

In 1910, Roald Amundsen borrowed Fridtjof Nansen’s ship Fram, originally planning an expedition the North Pole. 

At the last minute, Amundsen changed his mind and secretly set his sights on the South Pole instead. He revealed the true destination to his crew only after leaving their last port of call (!).

Amundsen’s approach was defined by meticulous preparation, experience from the Arctic, expert use of skis and dogs for transport, and lightweight equipment.

Scott’s British expedition relied on ponies (lol) that did not survive, man-hauling sledges, and motors that failed in the extreme cold, while carrying heavier scientific equipment.

Amundsen and his team reached the South Pole on 14 December 1911 and returned safely.

Scott arrived about a month later, on 17 January 1912 … only to find the Norwegian flag already flying 🇳🇴

Tragically, Scott and his men perished on the return journey.

In Amundsen’s own words, “Victory awaits him who has everything in order - luck, people call it. Defeat is certain for him who has neglected to take the necessary precautions in time; this is called bad luck.”
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Now that the US is knocked out, I am formally extending an invitation to the American people to support Norway.

Why?

1: The Vikings discovered America before Columbus.

2: There are more ethnic Norwegians in the US than in Norway.

3: Next weekend we can pillage the English https://t.co/Ex8hGfSFR8
tweets.full_text:
In addition to absolutely crushing the English in the Viking age, the Norwegians also massively owned the English in polar expeditions.

Here is the story of Amundsen v Scott.

In 1910, Roald Amundsen borrowed Fridtjof Nansen’s ship Fram, originally planning an expedition the North Pole. 

At the last minute, Amundsen changed his mind and secretly set his sights on the South Pole instead. He revealed the true destination to his crew only after leaving their last port of call (!).

Amundsen’s approach was defined by meticulous preparation, experience from the Arctic, expert use of skis and dogs for transport, and lightweight equipment.

Scott’s British expedition relied on ponies (lol) that did not survive, man-hauling sledges, and motors that failed in the extreme cold, while carrying heavier scientific equipment.

Amundsen and his team reached the South Pole on 14 December 1911 and returned safely.

Scott arrived about a month later, on 17 January 1912 … only to find the Norwegian flag already flying 🇳🇴

Tragically, Scott and his men perished on the return journey.

In Amundsen’s own words, “Victory awaits him who has everything in order - luck, people call it. Defeat is certain for him who has neglected to take the necessary precautions in time; this is called bad luck.”

tweets.text:
So just to recap the Grass call

- H1 2026 rev = 2025 full year rev which is ~$17M
- H1 2025 rev = $2.7M — ~6x YoY increase
- Node rewards paid in USDC instead of GRASS
- There's a shift coming from selling training data to serving real-time data during AI inference, they call this "LCR"

Grass shows promising rev growth and is transforming into a decentralized version of Exa/Firecrawl/Tavily but the token dumped 30%+ in a day because there's nothing concrete about token value accrual

Not sure if bullish or bearish but interesting developments indeed....
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Kicking off the hunt for token-aligned onchain AI teams
​
Have a few candidates in mind rn - will drop the research this week
​
Thoughts? Which AI team do you think is most aligned?
tweets.full_text:
So just to recap the Grass call

- H1 2026 rev = 2025 full year rev which is ~$17M
- H1 2025 rev = $2.7M — ~6x YoY increase
- Node rewards paid in USDC instead of GRASS
- There's a shift coming from selling training data to serving real-time data during AI inference, they call this "LCR"

Grass shows promising rev growth and is transforming into a decentralized version of Exa/Firecrawl/Tavily but the token dumped 30%+ in a day because there's nothing concrete about token value accrual

Not sure if bullish or bearish but interesting developments indeed....

tweets.text:
alright hats-off to meta the way this new model works is v cool - the longer it thinks the better the image/video:

> the model doesn't just generate an image, it refines it by using editing tools, reviewing it then refining it even more - test-time compute for an image model!

> its equipped with web and coding tools to search the internet and get a better understanding of what to  create

> draws from instagram image data to get additional context for your prompt

they might have solved the taste problem simply by forcing the model to think for longer 

Jiahui built this at openAI with their o-series models and now doing the same for image models

love it
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Today we're launching Muse Image and previewing Muse Video, our next Multimodal models after Muse Spark!

Generation goes Agentic and it's way more fun and powerful: it searches, codes, self-reflects, and calls tools to get things right. It's also freeform, interleaving text and https://t.co/uZuOVbElNa
